  • Load the Dataset
  • Salary Distribution by Employment Type
  • Salary Distribution by Industry
  • Job Posting Trends Over Time
  • Top 10 Job Titles by Count
  • Remote vs On-Site Job Postings
  • Skill Demand Analysis by Industry
  • Salary Analysis by ONET Occupation Type

Module 04 Lab 01: Visual Reporting and Storytelling

Author

Zhiyang Guo

Load the Dataset

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.io as pio

pio.renderers.default = "notebook_connected"

spark = (
    SparkSession.builder
    .appName("LightcastData")
    .config("spark.driver.host", "localhost")
    .getOrCreate()
)

df = (
    spark.read
    .option("header", "true")
    .option("inferSchema", "true")
    .option("multiLine", "true")
    .option("escape", "\"")
    .csv("./data/lightcast_job_postings.csv")
)

df.printSchema()
df.show(5)

-----+-------+--------------------+-------+---------------+-------+---------------+-----------------+----------------------+------------+--------------------+------------+--------------------+------------+--------------------+------------+--------------------+------------+--------------------+
only showing top 5 rows

Salary Distribution by Employment Type

salary_employment_pd = (
    df
    .filter((df.SALARY_FROM.isNotNull()) & (df.SALARY_FROM > 0))
    .select("EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME", "SALARY_FROM")
    .toPandas()
)

fig = px.box(
    salary_employment_pd,
    x="EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME",
    y="SALARY_FROM",
    color="EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME",
    title="Salary Distribution by Employment Type",
    labels={
        "EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME": "Employment Type",
        "SALARY_FROM": "Salary From"
    },
    template="plotly_white"
)

fig.update_layout(
    title_font_size=22,
    font=dict(size=14),
    xaxis_tickangle=-30,
    showlegend=False
)

fig.show()
[Stage 85:>                                                         (0 + 1) / 1]

This box plot compares salary distributions across employment types using the salary-from field. The graph helps reveal whether full-time, part-time, and mixed employment categories show different compensation ranges and salary variability.

Salary Distribution by Industry

salary_industry_pd = (
    df
    .filter((df.SALARY_FROM.isNotNull()) & (df.SALARY_FROM > 0) & (df.NAICS2_NAME.isNotNull()))
    .select("NAICS2_NAME", "SALARY_FROM")
    .toPandas()
)

fig = px.box(
    salary_industry_pd,
    x="NAICS2_NAME",
    y="SALARY_FROM",
    color="NAICS2_NAME",
    title="Salary Distribution by Industry",
    labels={
        "NAICS2_NAME": "Industry",
        "SALARY_FROM": "Salary From"
    },
    template="plotly_white"
)

fig.update_layout(
    title_font_size=22,
    font=dict(size=13),
    xaxis_tickangle=-45,
    showlegend=False
)

fig.show()
[Stage 86:>                                                         (0 + 1) / 1]

This box plot compares salary distributions across industries using NAICS2 industry names. The visualization shows how compensation ranges differ across broad industries and helps identify industries with higher salary variation.

Job Posting Trends Over Time

posting_trends_pd = (
    df
    .filter(df.POSTED.isNotNull())
    .groupBy("POSTED")
    .count()
    .withColumnRenamed("count", "job_count")
    .orderBy("POSTED")
    .toPandas()
)

posting_trends_pd["POSTED"] = pd.to_datetime(posting_trends_pd["POSTED"])

fig = px.line(
    posting_trends_pd,
    x="POSTED",
    y="job_count",
    title="Job Posting Trends Over Time",
    labels={
        "POSTED": "Posted Date",
        "job_count": "Number of Job Postings"
    },
    template="plotly_white"
)

fig.update_traces(line=dict(width=3, color="#2E86AB"))
fig.update_layout(
    title_font_size=22,
    font=dict(size=14)
)

fig.show()
[Stage 87:>                                                         (0 + 1) / 1]

This line chart shows how job postings fluctuate across posting dates. Peaks indicate dates with higher posting activity, while lower points show periods when fewer postings were recorded.

Top 10 Job Titles by Count

top_titles_pd = (
    df
    .filter(df.TITLE_NAME.isNotNull())
    .groupBy("TITLE_NAME")
    .count()
    .withColumnRenamed("count", "job_count")
    .orderBy("job_count", ascending=False)
    .limit(10)
    .toPandas()
)

fig = px.bar(
    top_titles_pd,
    x="TITLE_NAME",
    y="job_count",
    color="job_count",
    title="Top 10 Job Titles by Count",
    labels={
        "TITLE_NAME": "Job Title",
        "job_count": "Job Count"
    },
    template="plotly_white",
    color_continuous_scale="Blues"
)

fig.update_layout(
    title_font_size=22,
    font=dict(size=14),
    xaxis_tickangle=-45,
    showlegend=False
)

fig.show()
[Stage 95:>                                                         (0 + 1) / 1]

This bar chart identifies the most frequently posted job titles in the dataset. The highest bars represent roles with the strongest posting volume and therefore the greatest observed demand.

Remote vs On-Site Job Postings

remote_pd = (
    df
    .filter(df.REMOTE_TYPE_NAME.isNotNull())
    .groupBy("REMOTE_TYPE_NAME")
    .count()
    .withColumnRenamed("count", "job_count")
    .toPandas()
)

fig = px.pie(
    remote_pd,
    names="REMOTE_TYPE_NAME",
    values="job_count",
    title="Remote vs On-Site Job Postings",
    template="plotly_white",
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2
)

fig.update_layout(
    title_font_size=22,
    font=dict(size=14)
)

fig.show()
[Stage 98:>                                                         (0 + 1) / 1]

This pie chart compares the share of postings across remote work categories. It helps show whether the job market in this dataset is dominated by on-site, remote, hybrid, or unspecified work arrangements.

Skill Demand Analysis by Industry

from pyspark.sql.functions import explode, split, regexp_replace, trim, col

skills_industry_pd = (
    df
    .filter(df.SKILLS_NAME.isNotNull() & df.NAICS2_NAME.isNotNull())
    .select(
        "NAICS2_NAME",
        explode(split(regexp_replace("SKILLS_NAME", r'[\[\]\n"]', ""), ",")).alias("skill")
    )
    .withColumn("skill", trim(col("skill")))
    .filter(col("skill") != "")
    .groupBy("NAICS2_NAME", "skill")
    .count()
    .withColumnRenamed("count", "skill_count")
    .orderBy("skill_count", ascending=False)
    .limit(50)
    .toPandas()
)

top_industries = skills_industry_pd["NAICS2_NAME"].value_counts().head(5).index
skills_industry_pd = skills_industry_pd[skills_industry_pd["NAICS2_NAME"].isin(top_industries)]

fig = px.bar(
    skills_industry_pd,
    x="NAICS2_NAME",
    y="skill_count",
    color="skill",
    title="Skill Demand Analysis by Industry",
    labels={
        "NAICS2_NAME": "Industry",
        "skill_count": "Skill Count",
        "skill": "Skill"
    },
    template="plotly_white"
)

fig.update_layout(
    title_font_size=22,
    font=dict(size=13),
    xaxis_tickangle=-30,
    barmode="stack"
)

fig.show()
[Stage 101:>                                                        (0 + 1) / 1]

This stacked bar chart compares frequently mentioned skills across major industries. The visualization highlights which skills appear most often in job postings and how skill demand varies by industry.

Salary Analysis by ONET Occupation Type

onet_salary_pd = (
    df
    .filter(
        df.ONET_NAME.isNotNull() &
        df.SALARY.isNotNull() &
        (df.SALARY > 0)
    )
    .groupBy("ONET_NAME")
    .count()
    .withColumnRenamed("count", "job_count")
    .orderBy("job_count", ascending=False)
    .limit(20)
    .toPandas()
)

salary_pd = (
    df
    .filter(
        df.ONET_NAME.isNotNull() &
        df.SALARY.isNotNull() &
        (df.SALARY > 0)
    )
    .select("ONET_NAME", "SALARY")
    .toPandas()
)

median_salary = (
    salary_pd
    .groupby("ONET_NAME")["SALARY"]
    .median()
    .reset_index()
)

onet_salary_pd = onet_salary_pd.merge(
    median_salary,
    on="ONET_NAME",
    how="left"
)

fig = px.scatter(
    onet_salary_pd,
    x="ONET_NAME",
    y="SALARY",
    size="job_count",
    color="SALARY",
    title="Salary Analysis by ONET Occupation Type",
    labels={
        "ONET_NAME": "ONET Occupation",
        "SALARY": "Median Salary",
        "job_count": "Number of Job Postings"
    },
    template="plotly_white",
    color_continuous_scale="Viridis"
)

fig.update_layout(
    title_font_size=22,
    font=dict(size=13),
    xaxis_tickangle=-45
)

fig.show()
[Stage 104:>                                                        (0 + 1) / 1]                                                                                [Stage 107:>                                                        (0 + 1) / 1]

This Sankey diagram shows flows between broad SOC occupation groups and more specific SOC occupation categories. Thicker flows represent more job postings associated with that career pathway relationship.
